food_subgroup: Stir-fried prawn dish with chilli and aromatic spices

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for spicy stir-fried prawn dish; closest parent 'shrimp food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for spicy stir-fried prawn preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144002 'Prawn - food (substance)' used as closest parent; no specific code for spicy fragrant prawn dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of spicy fragrant prawns is approximately 300 g, comprising whole or shell-on prawns stir-fried with chilli, garlic, and aromatics, usually shared as part of a multi-dish Chinese meal.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15152) adapted with stir-fry sauce and oil additions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base prawn values derived from USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180). Adjustments applied for stir-fry preparation: added cooking oil contribution (~5 g fat per 100 g dish), soy/oyster sauce sodium contribution (~350 mg sodium per 100 g dish), and minor carbohydrate from sauce (~2 g per 100 g). Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. This is a medium-confidence adapted estimate; no exact HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry for this specific dish was identified.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spicy fragrant prawns. Prawns and other shellfish are very low in digestible carbohydrate (approximately 4.5 g per 100 g in this dish, primarily from sauce). GI estimated at 5 by analogy with other low-carbohydrate seafood dishes and sauce-based preparations documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (5 × 4.5 × 3) / 100 = 0.68, rounded to 0.7. This is a low-GI, low-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1560 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sauce or low-sodium soy sauce preparation, as a single restaurant serving can contribute over 60% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~4.5 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~5), resulting in minimal postprandial glucose impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Spicy fragrant prawns are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic load; however, the dish should be consumed with attention to total meal carbohydrate from accompanying rice or noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol (~152 mg per 100 g) and moderate sodium; partially offset by omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate intake due to the high cholesterol content of prawns and the high sodium from the sauce, while noting that the low saturated fat content and presence of polyunsaturated fats are relatively favourable; portion control and low-sodium preparation are advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Fragrant Prawns (香辣虾, Xiāng là xiā) is a popular Chinese stir-fried seafood dish featuring whole or shelled prawns cooked in a wok over high heat with chilli peppers, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, producing a bold, aromatic, and spicy flavour profile. The dish is characterised by its intense umami depth from fermented sauces, the heat of fresh or dried chilli, and the natural sweetness of fresh prawns. Texturally, the prawns are firm and succulent with a lightly caramelised exterior from wok hei (breath of the wok). In Singapore, this dish is commonly served in Chinese zi char (煮炒) restaurants and is typically shared as part of a multi-dish family-style meal. Nutritionally, spicy fragrant prawns are a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 148 kcal, 14.5 g protein, 8.2 g fat, and 4.5 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is high (~520 mg per 100 g) due to soy and oyster sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is very low (~5), making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments. Prawns are a good source of selenium, vitamin B12, phosphorus, and iodine. The dish contains shellfish and soy allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in 香辣虾?
Is spicy fragrant prawn dish healthy?
How much sodium in Chinese spicy prawns?
Is 香辣虾 high in cholesterol?
Spicy prawn dish Singapore zi char

confidence_reason: No exact entry for spicy fragrant prawns was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp data (FDC ID 175180) with adjustments for stir-fry oil and sauce contributions. GI is an analogous estimate based on the low carbohydrate content of the dish and comparable seafood preparations in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Food name corrected from input 'Spicy Fragrant Pranw' (apparent typo) to canonical English 'Spicy Fragrant Prawns'. Chinese name 香辣虾 preserved from input. 2. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) with stir-fry preparation adjustments; no exact Singapore-specific database entry was identified. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for cooked shrimp and is not expected to be significant in this preparation. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in cooked shrimp and vegetable oil stir-fry preparation per USDA data. 5. GI set to 5 as an analogous estimate for a very low-carbohydrate seafood stir-fry dish; gi_category is low. Glycemic load per 300 g serving is 0.7. 6. Sodium is high due to soy sauce and oyster sauce in the recipe; individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should exercise portion control. 7. This dish contains shellfish (prawns) and soy allergens; not suitable for shellfish-allergic individuals. 8. Halal status is conditionally true assuming halal-certified sauces and no pork-derived ingredients; restaurant-specific verification is advised. 9. Price data from field survey data (SGD 41.60) is preserved as contextual metadata but not used in nutritional analysis. 10. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g represent nutrients not reported in the adapted USDA source for this dish preparation.
